title: Anger Management the Jewish Way: What the Rambam Taught
title_zh: 犹太式愤怒管理:拉姆班教导的智慧
title_he: ניהול כעסים בדרך היהודית: מה לימד הרמב"ם

sources.title: Mishneh Torah, Hilchot De'ot 2:3
sources.author: Maimonides (Rambam)
sources.description: The Rambam's teaching on anger as an extremely bad trait requiring complete avoidance

sources.title: Talmud Bavli, Nedarim 22b
sources.description: The dangers of anger and how it pushes away the Divine Presence

sources.title: The Analects of Confucius
sources.author: Confucius
sources.description: Chinese parallel teachings on controlling anger and thinking of consequences

sources.title: Everyday Holiness: The Jewish Spiritual Path of Mussar
sources.author: Alan Morinis
sources.description: Modern application of Mussar practices including anger management

concepts: Confucian teaching: 'When anger rises, think of the consequences' (Analects 12:21)
Daoist concept of wu wei (effortless action) - responding with calm flexibility
Harmony (he) as a supreme value in Chinese culture paralleling Jewish shalom
Confucian emphasis on self-cultivation (修身) before influencing others
Daoist 'water' metaphor - flexibility and adaptation over force

key_concepts.definition: Anger - considered by the Rambam to be an extremely bad trait requiring complete avoidance
Character traits - the subject of constant refinement in Jewish ethics
The middle path - the Rambam's general principle for character traits, with anger being the exception
Jewish ethical and character development tradition
Idolatry - the Rambam compares chronic anger to this because it replaces God with ego

target_audience: Chinese native Mandarin speakers with no prior Jewish knowledge
